title: Bootstrapping Democracy: Transforming Local Governance and Civil Society in Brazil By Gianpaolo Baiocchi, Patrick Heller and Marcelo K. Silva Stanford University Press. 2011. 224 pages. $21.95 paper
abstract: Creativity. Experimentation. Adaptation. Building new democratic institutions needs heavy doses of ingenuity and risk taking to take advantage of the impulses for change coming from civil society and public officials. Bootstrapping Democracy, written by Gianpaolo Baiocchi, Patrick Heller and Marcelo K. Silva, demonstrates the importance of ongoing experimentation as local governments and their civil society allies seek to deepen the quality of democratic institutions. The “bootstrapping” metaphor illuminates how democratic reformers must pull themselves over the local constraints of political exclusion, clientelism, apathy and a hierarchical political culture to initiate new political projects. The experimentation doesn't occur in a vacuum, but is built on the existing configuration of civil society as well as electoral politics. A crucial lesson provided by Bootstrapping Democracy is that both new democratic institutions and civil society are malleable; both can be adapted at the local level to deepen the quality of democratic practices. The authors' empirical focus is one of the world's best-known participatory experiments, Brazil's Participatory Budgeting (PB), which is a year-long participatory policymaking process through which citizens have the right to make binding decisions regarding the allocation of public funds. Their research design, a paired case comparison, advances academic and policy debates on participatory democracy in two ways. First, their approach allows us to better understand the effect of PB in Brazilian municipalities that are very different than Porto Alegre, thus greatly enriching our comparative knowledge. Second, and more importantly, their paired case comparison allows them to show how PB programs produce political and social change that is distinct from similar municipalities that choose not to adopt PB. A common critique of PB programs is that the changes associated with PB programs would have happened regardless of whether the city adopted PB. The findings presented in Bootstrapping Democracy demonstrate that civil society and the local state change as a result of the adoption of PB programs. The comparative approach used in this book makes it useful for both undergraduate and graduate courses.
